title: Cobweb felt
subtitle: Ultra-thin felted fabric made from a single wisp-layer of fine merino or Corriedale
excerpt: Cobweb felt is felted fabric so thin it is translucent when held to the light. A single whisper-thin layer of fine merino or Corriedale is laid, wetted out, and felted until the fibres lock into a stable gossamer cloth. The technique is used for scarves, lampshades, and artwork overlays. The critical skill is laying an even web thin enough to felt through but without holes.
sourceNotes: Cobweb felting is an established specialist technique in contemporary textile practice. Method draws on standard wisp-layer technique as taught in advanced felting workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: An ultra-thin felted fabric laid from a single layer of very fine, widely spaced roving wisps. The finished cloth is semi-translucent and extremely lightweight.

A felting technique in which roving is drawn out to its finest, most translucent state before laying. Each wisp is only a few fibres wide; wisps are placed end to end and side by side to form a continuous but very thin layer.

content.text: Cobweb felt demands fine merino (18-21 micron) or fine Corriedale. Coarser fibres do not lock reliably at this density and produce felt with obvious holes rather than a gossamer cloth. The
: roving pulled so thin you can read print through it, laid in overlapping wisps to form a single continuous sheet. A sheet 50 x 50 cm pre-felt uses only about 8-10 g of roving.

items.name: Hot water and cold water access
Felting net or old sheer curtain
Pool noodle or large rolling pin

content.text: Pull a length of merino roving apart at an angle until you have a strand roughly 3-4 fibres wide. Lay these wisps side by side on the bamboo mat, each wisp at a slight angle to the next, overlapping by about 1 cm. The layer should be uniform: when you hold it to the light there should be no thick patches or dark blobs, but no large open holes either. A few tiny holes are acceptable. Lay a second wisp layer at 45 degrees to the first.

Place the felting net over the layer. Pour hot soapy water from a height so it falls as a gentle shower over the net. Do not pour directly onto the roving or it will disturb the wisps. Press down with flat palms through the net until everything is saturated. Begin rubbing through the net in extremely gentle circular strokes. If fibres bunch under the net, slow down.

Once the surface holds without the net (test a corner), roll the piece in the bamboo mat over a pool noodle for firmness. Roll 30-40 passes, then unroll and check. The piece should show clear shrinkage by the end of the second roll session. Because the cloth is thin, it felts faster than a standard panel. Over-rolling produces a stiff board rather than a drape; check regularly.

Rinse in warm water, then a cold-water plunge. Press out moisture in a towel. Stretch the wet cloth gently to the desired final shape and pin to a foam board. Do not hang cobweb felt to dry: it will stretch under its own negligible weight and distort.

items.problem: The layer tears or develops large holes during wetting
items.cause: Water poured directly onto the roving displaced the wisps before they locked
items.fix: Always pour through the felting net and keep the stream at a height of at least 30 cm to reduce impact.

items.problem: The finished cloth is stiff rather than drapy
items.cause: Over-rolling or too many fulling passes compacted the thin cloth too firmly
items.fix: Check for drape after each rolling session. Stop as soon as the fibres hold together and pass the pinch test: you should be just barely unable to separate them.

items.problem: Thick blobs remain visible in the finished cloth
items.cause: The wisps were not pulled thin enough or overlapped excessively in places
items.fix: In the next piece, hold each wisp to the light before laying it. If you cannot see through it, pull it thinner.
